interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Mississippi Freedom Summer Volunteer and MFDP Researcher
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

main_summary.summary:
Lisa Anderson Todd recounts her path from a 1950s suburban upbringing in Summit, New Jersey to participation in the 1964 Mississippi Freedom Summer and decades-long research on the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party (MFDP) convention challenge at Atlantic City. Daughter of a corporate lawyer and a psychiatric social worker, Todd describes parents who were not particularly liberal but engaged in public service through their Lutheran church and the United Negro College Fund. After a 1962 Experiment in International Living summer in northern Sweden, Todd chose to spend the summer of 1963 in the American South rather than return to Europe, signing up for an Ecumenical Voluntary Service project at Tougaloo College and a subsequent American Friends Service Committee voter-registration project in Greensboro, North Carolina, where she helped register 803 people. At Tougaloo she met Reverend Ed King, Memphis Norman, Dave Dennis of CORE, and Jane Stembridge of SNCC; on August 1, 1963 she traveled with a small group to the SNCC office in Greenwood at Bob Moses's invitation, then to Ruleville where she had dinner at Fannie Lou Hamer's home and heard Hamer recount the Winona jail beating.

Todd applied to SNCC for summer work in fall 1963 and was accepted without an interview based on her prior summer record. She arrived late to the Oxford, Ohio orientation, in time to hear Bob Moses tell volunteers their job was to tell their congressmen and local newspapers what was happening in Mississippi so federal protection could be obtained. She was assigned to voter registration in Greenville, Mississippi -- a relatively moderate Delta city -- under project director George Rawick (later Muriel Tillinghast), with Charles E. Cobb Jr. as the regional project director for Washington and Issaquena counties. Canvassing in interracial teams, she gathered Freedom Registration forms for the MFDP, worked precinct meetings, and attended the Second Congressional District caucus in Greenville, where Fannie Lou Hamer led the singing. Todd helped raise funds for what became the Herbert Lee Memorial Community Center in Greenville. She and her friend Pat Vail drove to Atlantic City for the Democratic National Convention, sleeping at the Union Baptist Temple Church where MFDP delegation meetings were held, walking the boardwalk to lobby state delegates, and witnessing the rejection of the two-seat 'compromise' offer.

After the summer, Todd returned home, entered Stanford Law School in fall 1964, and clerked the following summer in Mississippi with the National Lawyers Guild under Claudia Shropshire Morcom, surveying the need for Title III public-facility desegregation litigation. Her career moved through the United Planning Organization in Washington (under executive director Wiley A. Branton Sr.), private government-contracts practice, and ultimately appointment as an administrative judge on the NASA Board of Contract Appeals. After retiring in April 2006, Todd devoted herself to researching the 1964 MFDP convention challenge -- consulting the LBJ Library Johnson tapes, Anne Romaine's oral interviews at UNC, and documents implicating Walter Reuther, Hubert Humphrey, Joseph L. Rauh Jr., Bayard Rustin, and Representative Charles Diggs Jr. in the simultaneous-meeting maneuver that produced the at-large two-seat offer the MFDP rejected. Todd argues that Johnson's press conference timed against Hamer's Credentials Committee testimony was pre-planned rather than hastily called, that Walter Reuther rather than Hubert Humphrey was the decisive operator on Tuesday of the convention week, and that the work of many ordinary participants -- not a few leaders -- accomplished MFDP's longer-term reforms of the Democratic Party and the passage of the Voting Rights Act.
key_themes: Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party convention challenge
White volunteer experience and self-positioning in the Movement
Religious and ecumenical institutional support for the Movement
Memory, research, and the rewriting of personal historical narrative
Gender, race, and authority on civil rights projects
Local versus traditional leadership in Delta organizing
main_summary.historical_significance: Todd's interview is one of the more analytically reflective Freedom Summer volunteer accounts in the Civil Rights History Project corpus, combining the immediate participant memory of Greenville voter registration with the perspective of a researcher who has spent nearly a decade reconstructing the 1964 Atlantic City convention challenge from primary sources. Her account complicates standard narratives of the MFDP 'compromise' -- naming Walter Reuther, Bayard Rustin, and the simultaneous-meeting maneuver as the operative mechanisms -- and preserves first-person memory of organizing in a relatively moderate Delta city (Greenville) that is less represented in the secondary literature than the dramatic Greenwood, Ruleville, and Hattiesburg accounts. Her recollections of Bob Moses, Fannie Lou Hamer, Reverend Ed King, Charles E. Cobb Jr., Jane Stembridge, Hodding Carter, and Johnny Frazier offer texture that supplements organizational-history sources.

errors: Summary states Todd's group helped register 803 people in Greensboro, NC; transcript supports this figure (segment 93) as Todd's recollection but it is presented in the summary without the speaker's own qualifier 'we were able to' which marked it as a project-team accomplishment, not Todd individually.

Summary states the Atlantic City church was 'Union Baptist Temple Church'; the transcript renders the church name as 'Union Baptist Temple Church' (segment 967) which Todd states from memory but no independent verification appears in the transcript itself.
improvements: Use Todd's exact framing for the MFDP-vs-compromise rejection: she emphasizes there was 'no compromise' (segment 986) because there were not two negotiating sides, and the summary should preserve that interpretive point.
Add Todd's late-life finding that there were only ~500 college-age volunteers, not 1,000 (segments 1270-1273), as a corrective contribution.
Make explicit the distinction between Todd's contemporaneous memory and her later research findings; the interview repeatedly foregrounds this distinction (segments 444-447, 1134-1137).

chapters.title: Family Background and Suburban New Jersey Upbringing
chapters.summary: Todd opens the interview by describing her family of origin: father Carl Anderson, a corporate lawyer who grew up poor as one of six children in Hibbing, Minnesota, and her mother, a psychiatric social worker also from Hibbing. The family moved to Summit, New Jersey after her father took a corporate job. Todd characterizes her parents as not particularly liberal -- her father was a Republican who remembered the McCarthy era and was wary of signing documents -- but interested in public service. She describes a typical 1950s suburban upbringing with two older brothers and a stay-at-home mother, attending an all-girls independent day school. Todd reflects that her father's poor childhood in Hibbing may have influenced her interest in the poor and excluded, and notes the ethnic-but-prosperous character of Hibbing (Slavs and Finns, the Iron Range mines, the well-equipped Hibbing High School). She acknowledges she had no meaningful exposure to local Black community members despite Black students attending her junior high school.

improvements: Could note Todd's specific phrase that her father was 'afraid of signing documents because of the McCarthy scare' (segment 28) for color.

chapters.title: Summer 1963: Tougaloo and the Path to Civil Rights Work
chapters.summary: Todd describes the formative experience that led her toward Freedom Summer: a summer with the Experiment in International Living in northern Sweden, after which she decided to research domestic work camps and chose Tougaloo, Mississippi over Brownsville, Texas for summer 1963. She participated in an Ecumenical Voluntary Service project at Tougaloo College doing maintenance work alongside Civil Rights Movement learning -- nonviolent training workshops, social-science-lab speakers, and a transformative August 1, 1963 trip to Greenwood at Bob Moses's invitation. The group split, and Todd traveled with a small contingent to Ruleville where they had dinner at Fannie Lou Hamer's home and heard Hamer tell the story of her beating in Winona. The Ruleville mass meeting that evening, with mostly women welcoming the visitors and singing 'We Shall Overcome,' was emotionally formative for Todd. After Tougaloo, Todd participated in an American Friends Service Committee voter-registration project in Greensboro, North Carolina, where eighteen volunteers lived in a church basement; the local registrars were cooperative and the group registered 803 people, mostly Democrats with some Republicans.
related_events: Fannie Lou Hamer Winona jail beating (June 1963)

errors: Todd describes Hamer's beating as occurring in Winona; she does not in this segment of the transcript specify the year, though it was June 1963.
improvements: Could quote Hamer's reception at Ruleville more vividly, including Todd's specific recollection of singing 'arm over arm, holding hands' (segment 79).

chapters.title: Tougaloo Encounters and Mississippi Movement Context
chapters.summary: Todd describes the movement figures she met at Tougaloo: Dave Dennis of CORE (whose answer about children in Birmingham demonstrations stayed with her), Memphis Norman who headed the social-science lab, Reverend Ed King with his scarred face from a previous attack, and Jane Schutt, a white Jackson woman doing interracial church work. She regrets not having met Dr. Ernst Borinski (away on vacation) or John Salter Jr. She mentions an evening at Dr. Daniel Beittel's home and a talk by Allan Knight Chalmers, who was pessimistic that Jackson would integrate quickly because it lacked Birmingham's external corporate-pressure structure. Todd dates her Tougaloo stay as approximately July 6 to August 3, 1963 -- arriving shortly after Medgar Evers's assassination, which she had read about in the New York Times before her trip. She recalls an alarming moment of seeing flame on her plane's engine before the flight to Jackson. Todd discusses the post-assassination state of Jackson organizing: the federal government had pressured Jackson to stop demonstrations and move toward voter registration; the NAACP had stopped providing bail; Charles Evers had taken over the NAACP; and the youth wing affiliated with John Salter Jr. and Medgar Evers had been at odds with the national NAACP.

improvements: Could include Dave Dennis's specific quoted answer that 'we want children to learn about how to stand up for freedom' (segment 137).
Add Todd's framing of Reverend Ed King's scarred face as reflecting 'the tremendous sacrifice he was making to be a part of the Civil Rights Movement as a white southerner' (segment 145).

chapters.title: Cornell, the Civil Rights Act Lobbying, and Application to SNCC
chapters.summary: Todd describes a difficult adjustment to Cornell University, where she ultimately pledged Kappa Alpha Theta sorority, which was rushing Jewish girls in violation of national rules -- a rule violation that later cost the chapter its national charter (it went local with the administration's support). She majored in American Studies and government, took a race relations sociology course with Robin M. Williams Jr., and worked one semester in the library her junior year. After her summer-of-1963 experiences, she became active in Cornell United Religious Work under Paul Gibbons (United Church of Christ representative), helping push for passage of the 1964 Civil Rights Act. Her roommate later told her she had returned to Cornell senior year 'a different person.' Todd met Jane Stembridge at Tougaloo, who was working on literacy books and connected Todd to SNCC literature -- though Todd never started a Friends of SNCC chapter as expected. The CURW group went to Washington over spring break and listened to Senate debate on the Civil Rights Act. Todd applied to work for SNCC in fall 1963, receiving a letter from Walter Tillow describing SNCC's planned summer project with Freedom Schools, legal resources, and voter registration. She had also taken the LSAT and applied to law school, initially debating during the summer whether to abandon law school to remain in Mississippi.
related_events: Cornell Fayette County, TN tent city project
Senate debate on the Civil Rights Act of 1964

improvements: Note Stembridge's specific characterization of Bob Moses to Todd as 'a saint' (segment 320).

chapters.title: Freedom Summer Orientation and Arrival in Greenville
chapters.summary: Todd describes parental dynamics around her decision to go to Mississippi: parents supported her, paid for by her own savings from working in a Newark department store. The family was already engaged with Black institutions through her father's role as corporate giving chair for the United Negro College Fund, where he sat next to playwright Lorraine Hansberry at one dinner. Todd recounts her arrival at the Oxford, Ohio orientation late (after attending a friend's wedding at Sage Chapel), where she heard Bob Moses, in his overalls and clean white t-shirt, instruct volunteers that just being present mattered, that they should not expect to accomplish much, and that they should write to congressmen and local newspapers to obtain federal protection because 'lives were being lost.' She remembers Vincent Harding's impressive remarks and Bruce Hanson reminding volunteers of their own potential prejudices. During nonviolence role-playing on the lawn, Karl Fleming of Newsweek punched Todd in the stomach (as a reporter participating in the exercise). The Schwerner-Chaney-Goodman disappearance happened on Sunday June 21 -- 'the day that I arrived in Mississippi,' Todd recalls -- changing the orientation mood. Todd was assigned to voter registration in Greenville, a relatively moderate Delta city, partly because of her Greensboro experience and partly because women's field work was considered dangerous in most Mississippi locations.
related_events: Oxford, Ohio orientation (Western College for Women)
Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner (June 21, 1964)

errors: Transcript states the date of the Schwerner/Chaney/Goodman disappearance as 'Saturday they disappeared' (segment 428) after Todd initially says 'Sunday, June, June 21'; this chapter summary follows Todd's initial date statement; in fact the three were murdered Sunday June 21, 1964. The transcript itself is ambiguous and the summary preserves Todd's own framing.
improvements: Quote Moses's specific phrase that volunteers' job was to obtain federal protection 'because lives were being lost' (segment 412).

chapters.title: Canvassing, Freedom Registration, and Living in Greenville
chapters.summary: Todd describes the Greenville project structure: George Rawick of the University of Chicago was project director for the first month before Muriel Tillinghast took over, with Charles E. Cobb Jr. as regional director for Washington, Sharkey, and Issaquena counties (Cobb traveled in the state and was one of the people who went to look for the missing civil rights workers). Todd canvassed in interracial teams (often with Pam Trotman, recruited by Stokely Carmichael from Howard) to avoid Black community deference to white volunteers. Initial housing was unstable: a first widow was uncomfortable hosting three white women; the second host, Mrs. Harden, took the group to church and was accused by neighbors of being run out by 'Uncle Toms' for doing so. Eventually Todd lived with Jerry and Lamar Britton, who owned a drugstore at North and Nelson and provided an air-conditioned, welcoming home. Voter registration was difficult under Mississippi's literacy-test-plus-public-name-publication regime; the workaround was the MFDP Freedom Registration form, which required only a signature and was kept private. Todd participated in precinct organization, county convention politics (where local businessman Mr. Chappell, a traditional Democratic Party figure, tried to control delegate selection), and the Second Congressional District caucus held in Greenville's Friendship Baptist Church (a former movie theater), where Fannie Lou Hamer led the singing and recognized Todd from the prior summer's visit.
related_events: Washington County / Issaquena County voter registration

errors: Transcript references 'Issaquena' and a third county that the speaker mishears or repeats ('Issaquena County County, and Issaquena County') -- the third county is Sharkey but Todd does not explicitly name it in the cited segment. The summary cautiously names Sharkey only because it is the canonical third county for that COFO region; this is a soft inference rather than a transcript-established fact.
improvements: Quote the specific moment of recognition between Hamer and Todd at the district caucus ('recalled my being at her home and she gave me a big hug,' segment 738).

chapters.title: MFDP Conventions, Local Politics, and Movement Tensions
chapters.summary: Todd describes the MFDP shadow-party process paralleling the official Democratic Party state machinery, with precinct, county, congressional district, and state conventions. The Second District caucus held in Greenville with Hamer leading the singing was a highlight. Todd was not at the state convention despite long believing she was (research into her diary corrected this memory). She discusses Charles E. Cobb Jr.'s later-recognized instruction to work around traditional leaders -- a practice she retrospectively identifies as 'empowerment,' though they did not use that term, just as they did not use Martin Luther King's term 'beloved community.' Todd describes tensions on the project: some Black SNCC-affiliated organizers spoke against Bob Moses (saying he was 'on his way out of the movement' and didn't understand current realities) and pushed for demonstrations against orientation instructions; a first picket at the federal building in Greenville (organized in response to the disappearance of the three civil rights workers) was tolerated by police; a second planned demonstration at the Black swimming pool was avoided after Todd and others argued for COFO Jackson approval. Todd recounts a Fourth of July barbecue at Dr. Matthew T. Frisbee's home for project volunteers, and the project's swimming at Hodding Carter's pool (arranged by Carter's nanny Dorothy Jones while the Carters were out), broken up when local men arrived on horseback to investigate. Barbara Mutnick and Nancy stayed overnight and Barbara wrote of finding a revolver in the nightstand drawer.
related_events: MFDP precinct, county, district, state conventions (summer 1964)

errors: Summary refers to Dorothy Jones working for the Hodding Carter family without explicit transcript confirmation of the term 'nanny'; Todd says Jones 'worked for the Hodding Carter family' (segment 708) and that 'she didn't want to be alone with the baby' (segment 902), which together support the inference. Summary uses 'nanny' as shorthand and the underlying transcript supports it.
improvements: Could include the transcript's specific quote that the activists were 'living in that near ideal state' (segment 830) as the closest the speakers came to 'beloved community' language.

chapters.title: Atlantic City Convention and the MFDP Challenge
chapters.summary: Todd recounts traveling with Pat Vail to the August 1964 Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City via a stop at her family home in New Jersey. They missed Fannie Lou Hamer's Saturday testimony (no television where they stayed), slept at the Union Baptist Temple Church where MFDP delegation meetings were held, and walked the boardwalk to lobby state delegates and join visuals and rallies outside convention hall. Todd was not in the room when the delegates voted to reject the two-seat 'compromise' but was in the church watching prominent figures including Senator Wayne Morse and Bayard Rustin urge acceptance. Martin Luther King was 'hushed out' of one such meeting and not allowed to speak; Representative Edith Green reportedly addressed the credentials supporters. Through her later research, Todd identifies how Walter Reuther's late-Tuesday arrival was the operative turning point: Reuther worked with the Walter Mondale subcommittee to produce the offer of two non-voting at-large seats with individually named delegates -- delegates who Mondale, with no contradiction from Joseph L. Rauh Jr., claimed Aaron Henry and Reverend Ed King had accepted (a claim King and Henry have not addressed on the record). The simultaneous-meeting maneuver -- the leadership tied up in one room while the credentials committee voted in another -- was conscious deception, not negotiation. Todd argues the Johnson press conference timed against Hamer's testimony was pre-planned (Johnson had announced it the day before with full camera coverage) rather than 'hastily called' as Joseph L. Rauh Jr.'s account propagated. Todd also describes Representative Charles Diggs Jr.'s request for the list of credentials committee MFDP supporters, given to Bob Moses and Courtland Cox -- a list that was then used overnight to threaten the named members into changing their votes. Reuther's letter offering to withdraw but warning the White House that, without him, the MFDP would be represented by 'commies' (Bill Kunstler and Arthur Kinoy) shows Reuther's dual role.
related_events: Fannie Lou Hamer Credentials Committee testimony (August 22, 1964)
Lyndon B. Johnson administration response to MFDP

errors: Summary attributes to Walter Reuther a specific arrival time ('late-Tuesday arrival'); transcript states Reuther 'arrived on Tuesday in the middle of the night' (segment 1111) which the summary collapses to 'late-Tuesday'.
Transcript phrasing has Todd say that LBJ called 'the governor of Iowa' (segment 1102) without giving a name; summary preserves this generality.
improvements: Foreground Todd's interpretive claim that 'there was no compromise' (segment 986) more centrally.
Preserve the specific datum that Reuther's letter said without him the MFDP would be represented by 'commies' (segment 1073) verbatim.

chapters.title: Law School, Mississippi 1965, and Disassociation from the Movement
chapters.summary: Todd transitions to her post-Atlantic City life: returning home, attending a fundraiser at Central Presbyterian Church in Summit with Mr. Schwerner and Chips Carter (a volunteer in Drew, Mississippi), where about 600 people attended; the money raised supported the Herbert Lee Memorial Freedom Center in Greenville. She drove across the country to Stanford Law School, lived with Karen Davis (sister of Ruleville Freedom School teacher Linda Davis), and joined the Law Students Civil Rights Research Council. She applied to return to Mississippi the next summer as a law clerk and was initially assigned to the Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law, but was reassigned after a Friday-afternoon meeting with attorney James Danner left her sounding 'rather militant.' She was assigned to the National Lawyers Guild and lived in Tougaloo with Claudia Shropshire Morcom, helping coordinate volunteer lawyers evaluating the need for Title III public-facility desegregation litigation (libraries, swimming pools, but not restaurants or public accommodations) in Madison County, Greenwood, Greenville, and Jackson. Todd disassociated from the movement during law school -- focusing on career, reading the San Francisco Chronicle, not getting involved in the women's or peace movements. After graduation she worked at the United Planning Organization in Washington under executive director Wiley A. Branton Sr.; later moved to Trobon Bauer and then Morgan, Lewis & Bockius in government contracts practice for nearly ten years; and eventually became an administrative judge first on the NASA Board of Contract Appeals and then on a merged Defense board.
keywords: lawyers committee for civil rights under law
related_events: Herbert Lee Memorial Community Center, Greenville
Title III Civil Rights Act facilities litigation
Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law

errors: Summary references 'Chips Carter (a volunteer in Drew, Mississippi)'; transcript renders the name as 'chips our wine' which is ambiguous (likely 'Chips Carter' or another volunteer). Summary preserves the most plausible reading but flags the uncertainty as an unsupported specific.
improvements: Preserve Todd's framing of the moment of disassociation: she described herself as not curious about Greenville on her return visit and 'I had become a different person, I was a law student' (paraphrasing segment 1194).
Add the detail that the Lawyers' Committee found her 'childish and immature' for not deferring (segment 1178).

chapters.title: Retirement, Research, and Writing on the MFDP Challenge
chapters.summary: Todd describes her decision to retire in April 2006 to write something more interesting than her work writing. A friend's question about her library of civil rights books prompted her to write a book. She first wrote a quick memoir from memory and her transcribed letters and diary, then shifted to focused research on the MFDP convention challenge -- consulting Taylor Branch's account, Anne Romaine's oral interviews at UNC, and the LBJ Library's Johnson tapes (online). She produced a 250-page research paper called 'New Perceptions Atlantic City Revisited' which she sent out before March 2008 surgery in New Orleans. John Dittmer advised her that her writing required footnotes, leading to a multi-year footnoting effort that revealed manuscript omissions. Mary King has mentored her writing with high standards. Todd reflects that the writing is much more difficult than expected, and that her experiences reshape her understanding -- recognizing 'we changed history' through the Voting Rights Act and Democratic Party reform (though not the 1964 Civil Rights Act, which preceded Freedom Summer). She notes that the work was done by 'many, many, many people' and corrects the common figure of 1,000 volunteers: there were only ~500 college-age volunteers; the remainder were professionals who came for short periods.
keywords: veterans of mississippi civil rights movement
related_events: Veterans of the Mississippi Civil Rights Movement reunions

improvements: Foreground Todd's specific claim that the election of Obama in 2008 is 'directly traced to the work that we did' (segment 1265) as her concrete connection-to-present claim.

chapters.title: Fear, the VFW Incident, and Closing Reflections
chapters.summary: In the closing minutes, Todd answers the question she had hoped to be asked: was she scared? She describes initial fear at the Freedom House in Greenville hearing cars circling -- which turned out to be police protecting the volunteers, since Greenville had no Klan or Citizens' Council. The standard concern was pickup trucks with rifle racks following cars on highways at night. Her closest brush with violence was at a VFW fundraiser dance organized by Charles Moore, where she danced with one Black man and went out on the porch with a second; the first man followed and pulled a knife, ending her interest in dating that summer. She walked across the street to a barbecue joint and waited for colleagues to take her back to the safe Black community. The interview closes with mutual thanks and Todd expressing she has enjoyed reflecting on these important memories.

improvements: Could note Todd's specific framing that Greenville was 'one of the safest places in the state' (segment 1321) and her reassurance from that assignment.

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Mississippi Freedom Summer Volunteer and MFDP Researcher
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

evidence: Clear chronological progression from family background through Tougaloo 1963, Cornell, Freedom Summer 1964, law school, and retirement research (Segment 1, 00:00:00,000 through Segment 1373, 02:49:23,000)
Effective thematic structure layering personal memory with research findings (Segment 1134, 02:11:17,000)
Interviewer Emilye Crosby provides strong organizing questions and follow-ups throughout
story_structure_coherence.justification: The interview follows a strong chronological arc with thematic loops back to research-uncovered facts. Some sections (particularly the long Atlantic City analytical chapter) sustain density without losing coherence. Minor backtracking occurs around the Greensboro/Mississippi sequence.

most_vivid_moment.quote: I can see the depth of the water in the bottom of the tub and the woman yells, don't use too much water.

evidence: Description of Fannie Lou Hamer's home reception with mostly women singing 'arm over arm, holding hands' (Segment 79, 00:09:22,000)
Specific identification of Bob Moses at orientation in overalls and clean white t-shirt (Segment 407, 00:47:48,000)
Recreated scene of Karl Fleming of Newsweek punching Todd in stomach during nonviolence role-play (Segment 422, 00:50:19,000)
Detailed description of the Britton drugstore at North and Nelson (Segment 574, 01:02:53,000)
Vivid VFW dance and knife incident (Segment 1334, 02:46:51,000)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Todd provides exceptionally specific details with full names of obscure local figures (Mr. Chappell, the Brittons, Dr. Matthew T. Frisbee, Pam Trotman) and verbatim quotes she records from her diary. Some moments are vividly cinematic; others are appropriately compressed.

most_authentic_moment.quote: And I said, what am I going to do? These people will expect that I go back home and forget. And I didn't want to forget because it meant a lot to me.

vulnerability_elements: Admits she 'lost her fury' and disassociated from the movement during law school (Segment 1188, 02:24:48,000)
Acknowledges she had no exposure to Black community in suburban New Jersey (Segment 33, 00:03:10,000)
Discusses her mother's distress over her staying in Mississippi (Segment 369-380)
Concedes her memory has been corrected by her own research multiple times (Segment 1135, 02:11:29,000)
emotional_authenticity.justification: Todd is intellectually candid about the limits of her contemporaneous memory and the gaps in her movement engagement. She demonstrates emotional range from awe (Hamer encounter) to fear (highway pickup trucks) to dispassionate self-criticism (her 'fury' loss during law school). She is somewhat reserved in expressing strong affect but never deflects difficult questions.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Todd combines disciplined narrative structure with research-corrected detail and a willingness to be self-critical. The interview is strongest when she alternates between sensory memory of Greenville organizing and the analytic findings of her retirement research on Atlantic City. Emotional moments are present but understated, in keeping with Todd's controlled, lawyerly affect.

major_events: Tougaloo summer 1963 / Greenwood SNCC office visit at Bob Moses's invitation (Segment 64, 00:07:06,000)
Dinner at Fannie Lou Hamer's home in Ruleville, August 1963 (Segment 66, 00:07:32,000)
Freedom Summer orientation at Oxford OH 1964 (Segments 405-424)
Greenville Mississippi voter registration / MFDP organizing summer 1964 (Segments 460-700)
Second Congressional District MFDP caucus, Greenville (Segments 731-735)

key_figures.name: Bob Moses
key_figures.relationship: Met at Tougaloo dance 1963; heard speak at Oxford orientation 1964

key_figures.name: Fannie Lou Hamer
key_figures.relationship: Dinner at Hamer's Ruleville home August 1963; reunited at Second District caucus 1964

key_figures.name: Charles E. Cobb Jr.
key_figures.relationship: Cobb gave Todd organizing direction during summer 1964

key_figures.name: Jane Stembridge
key_figures.relationship: Met at Tougaloo summer 1963; received one later letter

key_figures.name: Dave Dennis
key_figures.relationship: Met in Jackson summer 1963; received answer about Birmingham children's question

key_figures.name: George Rawick
key_figures.relationship: Greenville project director for first month summer 1964

key_figures.name: Hodding Carter
key_figures.relationship: Family connection through pool incident; later contact for research

organizational_involvement.role: Civil Rights Act lobbying group member
Precinct chair / canvasser / district caucus delegate
Summer law clerk (via Law Students Civil Rights Research Council)

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: Todd combines (a) ground-level participant memory of organizing in a relatively-moderate Delta city (Greenville) that is less mythologized than Greenwood/Ruleville/Hattiesburg in the secondary literature with (b) a researcher's documentary command of the 1964 Atlantic City convention challenge built over a decade of LBJ Library and Romaine-interview research. This combination is rare in the corpus.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Todd had direct contact with major figures (Hamer, Moses, Cobb, Stembridge, Ed King) and participated in the full MFDP organizing cycle from precinct through national convention. Her retirement-research has added a documentary layer to her firsthand recall that few other oral histories can match.

precision_examples: Quantitative claims: '803 people' registered in Greensboro (Segment 93, 00:10:45,000)
Specific addresses: drugstore at corner of North and Nelson (Segment 574)
Specific dates: 'March 2008' surgery in New Orleans (Segments 1040-1041)
Specific count: 600 people at the fundraiser at Central Presbyterian Church (Segment 1150)
verifiable_claims: Greenville voter registration project housed in Britton drugstore household; project directors Rawick then Tillinghast under regional director Charles E. Cobb Jr.
MFDP Second Congressional District caucus held at Friendship Baptist Church (former movie theater) with Fannie Lou Hamer leading singing
Atlantic City delegation slept at Union Baptist Temple Church
Reuther arrived in Atlantic City Tuesday in the middle of the night
Johnson press conference timed against Hamer's testimony was announced the day before
specificity_of_details.justification: Todd's specificity is exceptional in both names and dates, with multiple dates given to the day and many full names of obscure participants (Mr. Chappell, the Brittons, Mr. Williams, Charles Moore, Dr. Matthew Page). Her documentary research has armed her with verifiable claims that historians can cross-reference.

new_information: Greenville Mississippi's local MFDP organizing dynamics, including the role of traditional businessman Mr. Chappell in attempting to control delegate selection
The Herbert Lee Memorial Community Center as a post-Freedom-Summer infrastructure investment in Greenville
First-hand account of the Hodding Carter pool incident showing the social-network limits of Mississippi Delta liberalism
Documentary findings on the Reuther/Mondale subcommittee mechanics and Charles Diggs Jr.'s role in transmitting the credentials list
The pre-planned (not hastily-called) character of LBJ's press conference against Hamer's testimony
understudied_aspects: Greenville Mississippi (relatively moderate Delta city) as an organizing site under-represented vs Greenwood/Hattiesburg
Women's voter-registration field work (Greenville being one of few sites women could canvass)
The white-volunteer ecumenical-church pathway (Ecumenical Voluntary Service, AFSC, Cornell United Religious Work) into Mississippi organizing
The internal SNCC tensions in 1964 between Moses-aligned and anti-Moses voices in field offices
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Todd's research complicates the conventional 'Hubert Humphrey arranged the compromise to get the vice-presidency' framing by repositioning Walter Reuther as the operational architect, and complicates the 'Johnson hastily called the press conference' framing by showing it was pre-announced. Her insistence that 'there was no compromise' because there were not two negotiating sides also pushes back on standard narrative.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians studying the 1964 MFDP challenge can use Todd's findings to triangulate the Reuther role, the Diggs list, and the King/Henry acceptance question. Local historians of Washington County Mississippi can use her account of Greenville organizing as a counterweight to Greenwood-dominant Delta narratives.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: Todd's interview makes substantive new contributions to the historical record in multiple dimensions, both at the local-organizing level and at the national MFDP-challenge level. Her decade-long research yields documentary claims that materially complicate well-known narratives.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Todd's interview has unusually high historical value because it sits at the intersection of (a) direct participant memory in canonical 1964 events and (b) primary-source archival research that produces specific corrections to the standard secondary literature. The Greenville organizing texture and the Atlantic City research findings are both contributions historians can use directly.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: From suburban-New-Jersey sheltered teen with no Black peer relationships, through ecumenical exposure to Mississippi organizing and meaningful contact with Hamer and Moses, into a Freedom Summer voter-registration volunteer who briefly contemplated abandoning law school to remain in Mississippi; followed by a long period of career-focused disassociation from the movement, and a third-act transformation into a meticulous archivist of the MFDP challenge in retirement.
key_turning_points: Sweden summer (1962) decision not to vacation but to work in the US (Segment 54, 00:05:57,000)
Mass meeting at Ruleville with Hamer reception (Segment 69, 00:08:04,000)
Walter Tillow letter accepting her for Freedom Summer (Segment 342, 00:40:54,000)
Returning home to find mother distressed about her planned stay (Segment 367-371)
Decision to retire in April 2006 to write (Segment 1016, 02:29:36,000)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: Todd transitions from a 1950s Republican-suburban worldview through a brief but intense activist period, then to a closed-off legal career, then back to a third-life devoted to documenting the truth of the Atlantic City compromise. The arc is unusual in being both interrupted and recovered.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Inspirational primarily for its message that ordinary participants -- the 500 college-age volunteers, the local Greenville women -- 'changed history' through accumulated action rather than dramatic individual heroism (Segment 1257-1275). The retirement-research third act will inspire scholars and second-career writers.
transformation_growth.justification: Todd articulates a clear transformation arc but is more comfortable describing events than emotional reckonings. Her retirement-research arc is itself a moving form of self-completion, but the interview does not always foreground the emotional dimension of that recovery.

peak_moments.description: Knife pulled at VFW dance in Greenville
peak_moments.stakes: Personal physical danger; ended Todd's romantic engagement that summer

peak_moments.description: Hodding Carter pool party broken up by armed men on horseback
peak_moments.stakes: Implied potential vigilante reaction; revealed full-armed Carter household

peak_moments.description: Hearing about Schwerner-Chaney-Goodman disappearance the day of arrival
peak_moments.stakes: 'Are we all going to be now victimized?'

peak_moments.description: Rejection of the two-seat compromise at Atlantic City
peak_moments.stakes: MFDP delegation's national stand against the Johnson administration

tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From muted fear (highway pickup trucks) through analytical disappointment (Atlantic City compromise) to controlled triumph (knowing the long-term reforms followed)
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: The interview has several genuine peak moments but they are presented at a reflective remove. Todd's narrative tends toward analytical rather than dramatic, so the high-stakes content is real but understated.

universal_themes: Coming of age / leaving family expectations
Discovering vocation through immersion in unfamiliar community
The cost of commitment to social change
Memory, research, and the rewriting of one's own story
relatable_struggles: Navigating parents' anxiety about a daughter's risky choices
Choosing between competing careers (movement work vs law school)
Returning to old commitments after years of disengagement
human_moments: Discovering the bath water was being rationed (Segment 551, 01:01:08,000)
Mrs. Britton's hot-flash advice in a Mississippi summer (Segment 580-582)
Father's awkward conversation with Lorraine Hansberry (Segment 281-283)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Universal themes of coming-of-age, vocational choice, and late-life return to unfinished work make Todd's story relatable to college-age listeners considering volunteer service, mid-career listeners contemplating disengagement, and retirement-age listeners considering returning to early commitments.
relatable_experiences.justification: Todd's affect is reserved and analytical, which limits the emotional immediacy of relatable moments, but the structural themes of her life (coming-of-age, vocational discovery, mid-life pause, late-life recovery) are highly universal.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: The emotional resonance of Todd's interview is real but understated. Her affect is composed and analytical throughout, which makes the strongest moments (Ruleville, VFW knife, mother's distress) feel like punctuation rather than crescendos. The retirement-research arc carries significant emotional weight but is mostly presented as intellectual narrative.

barriers: Specialized Mississippi-organizing vocabulary (MFDP, COFO, Freedom Registration, precinct/county/district/state convention structure) is mostly self-contextualized but assumes some familiarity
Procedural detail of credentials committee mechanics requires close attention
language_clarity.justification: Todd's articulation is exceptionally clear and her lawyerly precision yields easy-to-follow sentences. She self-contextualizes most movement vocabulary; the few moments of unexplained insider language (Tougaloo, COFO, MFDP-vs-NAACP distinction) are minor.

slow_sections: Career chronology of administrative-judge work (Segments 1206-1228, 02:32:00,000 - 02:36:01,000) is detailed but slower
Stretches of detailed credentials-committee numerical research (Segments 1096-1133) demand audience focus
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: Engaged listeners can sustain attention through the full 2h49m, though the long Atlantic City analytical chapter (~25 minutes) is the most demanding stretch.
narrative_flow.justification: The interview maintains generally good momentum with distinct dramatic peaks. The slower analytical sections (Atlantic City mechanics, career chronology) are essential for content but somewhat drag the pacing. Interviewer Crosby's follow-up questions are well-placed and keep the narrative on track.

themes_connected: Voter suppression and democratic legitimacy (MFDP organizing as response to literacy tests and public-name publication)
The role of ordinary participants in social-movement outcomes ('we made a difference; if we made a difference, maybe it took a long time, but we made a difference' Segment 1275)
The Voting Rights Act of 1965 as Freedom Summer's true legislative legacy
Democratic Party reform from MFDP to the Obama election (Segment 1265)
Historical memory, research methodology, and the correction of secondary-source errors
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Todd explicitly connects her experience to contemporary civic-engagement themes (voting, encouraging young people to act, the dysfunction of the current Congress). Her closing remarks deliberately address young audiences (Segment 1278-1294).
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Yes; Todd takes specific care to articulate what Freedom Summer did and did not accomplish, and what the long-term legacy is. Her insistence on the '500 not 1,000' volunteer count and the 'many people, not a few leaders' framing is a meaning-making move.
broader_themes_connection.justification: Todd is unusually explicit about historical significance and contemporary relevance, in part because of her research orientation. She closes by encouraging young people to find passion, find allies, and organize -- a clear connection-to-present move.

accessibility.overall_assessment: Highly accessible interview for engaged listeners; the lawyerly clarity and contemporary-relevance framing compensate for the procedural density of the Atlantic City material. Audio quality and interviewer pacing are strong throughout.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: Lisa Anderson Todd's interview is a high-value oral history that combines vivid participant memory of 1964 Greenville organizing with a researcher's documentary command of the MFDP convention challenge. The interview is strongest on historical value (firsthand access combined with primary-source research) and accessibility (lawyerly clarity, explicit connection to contemporary themes); it is somewhat weaker on emotional resonance because Todd's affect is composed and analytical throughout. The retirement-research third act gives the interview a meta-historiographical dimension uncommon in the corpus -- Todd is simultaneously a witness to and a historian of the events she discusses.
key_strengths: Direct firsthand contact with major figures including Hamer, Moses, Cobb, Stembridge, and Ed King combined with documentary research that materially complicates standard narratives of the 1964 Atlantic City convention challenge
Exceptionally specific detail throughout (full names of obscure local figures, dates to the day, quantitative claims with citations) that historians can use directly
Self-critical and honest about the limits of her contemporaneous memory and the gaps in her movement engagement, modeling the discipline of memory-versus-research distinction
key_limitations: Emotional affect is reserved and lawyerly throughout, which limits the dramatic and emotional impact of the strongest moments
The long analytical chapter on the Atlantic City convention challenge is procedurally dense and demanding for general listeners, though essential for historical value

claims.claim: Lisa Anderson Todd was raised in Summit, New Jersey, daughter of corporate lawyer Carl Anderson and a psychiatric social worker mother
claims.supporting_excerpt: I'm the daughter of Corporate Lawyer, my father, Carl Anderson. And my mother was a psychiatric social worker. ... I grew up in Summit, New Jersey when my father got a corporate job in New Jersey. (Segments 13-16)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement of family background and location.

claims.claim: Todd participated in an Ecumenical Voluntary Service project at Tougaloo College in summer 1963
claims.supporting_excerpt: I participated in a project of the Ecumenical Voluntary Service at Tougaloo College. ... summer of 1963. (Segments 58-59)
claims.rationale: Direct statement establishing the organization, location, and year.

claims.claim: Todd had dinner at Fannie Lou Hamer's home in Ruleville on August 1, 1963 at Bob Moses's invitation
claims.supporting_excerpt: We had an incredible day on August 1, 1963, going up to Greenwood to the SNCC office at the invitation of Bob Moses. ... I guess there were three or four of us that went to Ruleville and had dinner at Fannie Lou Hamer's home. (Segments 64-66)
claims.rationale: Direct date, location, host, and invitation source all established by transcript.

claims.claim: Hamer told Todd's group the story of the Winona beating
claims.supporting_excerpt: And she told the story to us of the beating in Winona. (Segment 67)
claims.rationale: Direct quotation from Todd.

claims.claim: Todd's Greensboro AFSC project registered 803 people, mostly Democrats with some Republicans
claims.supporting_excerpt: And we were able to register 803 people, mostly Democrats, but some Republicans. (Segment 93)
claims.rationale: Direct quantitative claim from Todd.

claims.claim: Todd met Dave Dennis of CORE in Jackson summer 1963
claims.supporting_excerpt: I think Corps was largely doing the work in Jackson in 1963. I met Dave Dennis there. (Segments 131-132)
claims.rationale: Direct statement of meeting and context.

claims.claim: Memphis Norman was heading the social science lab at Tougaloo summer 1963
claims.supporting_excerpt: Memphis Norman, was heading the social science lab that summer. (Segment 140)
claims.rationale: Direct statement.

claims.claim: Reverend Ed King had a scarred face which Todd related to his work as a white southerner in the civil rights movement
claims.supporting_excerpt: But we spent a lot of time with Rev. Ed King. And I looked at his scarred face and thought that that was related to the tremendous sacrifice he was making to be a part of the Civil Rights Movement as a white southerner (Segments 144-145)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person observation.

claims.claim: Todd read about Medgar Evers's assassination in the New York Times before leaving home for Jackson
claims.supporting_excerpt: Yes. I read that story in the New York Times before I left home. (Segment 147)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement.

claims.claim: Todd's father served as corporate giving chairman for the United Negro College Fund and sat next to Lorraine Hansberry at a dinner
claims.supporting_excerpt: The other part that's interesting about my father is that he was asked to be the corporate giving chairman for the United Negro College Fund. ... At one dinner he sat next to Lorraine Hansberry. (Segments 278-281)
claims.rationale: Direct quotation from Todd.

claims.claim: Todd attended Cornell University and pledged Kappa Alpha Theta, which lost its national charter and went local after rushing Jewish girls
claims.supporting_excerpt: Our sorority was pledging Jewish girls at a time when the national did not expect that to be the case. And it turned out after I left that as a result of that Kappa Alpha Theta Cornell lost its charter and went local with the support of the Cornell administration. (Segments 237-238)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person account.

claims.claim: Todd took a race relations sociology course with Robin M. Williams Jr. at Cornell
claims.supporting_excerpt: I elected that course. Robin M. Williams Jr was a noted sociology professor at Cornell. (Segment 253)
claims.rationale: Direct identification of professor and course.

claims.claim: Todd applied to SNCC for summer work in fall 1963 and received a letter from Walter Tillow
claims.supporting_excerpt: I applied to Work for Snick in the fall of 63. And I got a letter from Walter Tillow that said, Snick is planning a project that will include Freedom Schools and legal resources and voter registration. (Segments 341-343)
claims.rationale: Direct statement of application and letter author.

claims.claim: Karl Fleming of Newsweek punched Todd in the stomach during nonviolence role-playing at the Oxford orientation
claims.supporting_excerpt: And I have a note in the postcard I sent home to my parents I guess that, or else I only have it in my memory but it's a distinct memory that Carl Fleming of Newsweek punched me in the stomach. (Segment 422)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person memory with corroborating note.

claims.claim: The Schwerner-Chaney-Goodman disappearance occurred on Sunday, June 21 (Todd's day of arrival in Mississippi)
claims.supporting_excerpt: Schwerner, a staff, of course, staff person and good men of volunteer just like me, not just like me of course, disappeared that Sunday, June, June 21. That's Saturday they disappeared. ... The day that I arrived in Mississippi. (Segments 427-430)
claims.rationale: Transcript shows confusion in the moment between Sunday and Saturday for the disappearance date. The summary preserves Todd's framing of 'June 21' as her arrival day; the historical date of the murders is June 21, 1964 (Sunday). The summary's date is correct but transcript wording is ambiguous; marking partially_supported out of caution.

claims.claim: George Rawick from the University of Chicago was Greenville project director for the first month, succeeded by Muriel Tillinghast
claims.supporting_excerpt: George Rawick was our project director. He was from the University of Chicago. He was a graduate student, I believe, and he was there for the first month. And then Muriel Tillinghast became the project director. (Segments 469-472)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement of directors and succession.

claims.claim: Charles E. Cobb Jr. was the regional project director for Washington, Sharkey, and Issaquena counties
claims.supporting_excerpt: He was the project director for Washington County, Issaquena County County, and Issaquena County, all three in the region. (Segment 476)
claims.rationale: Transcript identifies Washington and Issaquena explicitly; the third county (Sharkey, the canonical third county in COFO's regional grouping) is not named explicitly in this transcript -- the speaker mis-renders it as 'Issaquena County County'. Naming Sharkey is a soft inference, demoting to partially_supported.

claims.claim: Pam Trotman, a Howard graduate, was recruited to the Greenville project by Stokely Carmichael
claims.supporting_excerpt: We canvassed as a team, one black and one white, and Pam Trotman, I think I went with often. She was a graduate of Howard, and had been recruited by Stokely Carmichael to come down. (Segments 460-461)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement.

claims.claim: Todd lived with Jerry and Lamar Britton, who owned a drugstore at North and Nelson in Greenville
claims.supporting_excerpt: The three of us lived with Jerry and Lamar Britton. ... They owned a drugstore at the corner of North and Nelson. (Segments 568-574)
claims.rationale: Direct statement of housing and Britton business.

claims.claim: The Second Congressional District MFDP caucus was held at Friendship Baptist Church (a former movie theater) in Greenville, with Fannie Lou Hamer leading the singing
claims.supporting_excerpt: We met at the Friendship Baptist Church, which was formerly a movie theater. It was a big place. And they came in on Sunday and Fannie Lou Hamer led the singing. (Segments 732-734)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement of venue and Hamer role.

claims.claim: Todd and Pat Vail attended the 1964 Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City, sleeping at the Union Baptist Temple Church where MFDP delegation meetings were held
claims.supporting_excerpt: Pat and I went to our house, stayed overnight and drove down to Atlantic City and missed entirely Saturday, Fannie Lou Hamers testimony, didn't see it on television. ... stayed in the church of Union Baptist Temple Church where the meetings were held of the MFDP delegation. (Segments 966-967)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement of attendance, sleeping arrangement, and church name.

claims.claim: Martin Luther King was hushed/silenced and not allowed to speak at the Tuesday afternoon meeting at the church
claims.supporting_excerpt: I distinctly remember that Martin Luther King was not allowed to speak. That he was, he was hushed, hushed out. (Segment 997)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person memory.

claims.claim: Walter Reuther arrived in Atlantic City Tuesday in the middle of the night and worked with the Mondale subcommittee to produce the offer of two non-voting at-large seats with individually named delegates
claims.supporting_excerpt: But it all happened because of Walter Reuther, who arrived on Tuesday in the middle of the night. And he worked with the Mondale subcommittee of the Credentials Committee to come up with the president. ... that the delegates would be at large and that they would be individually named people. (Segments 1111-1112)
claims.rationale: Direct documentary-research-grounded statement.

claims.claim: Joseph L. Rauh Jr. wrote a letter to the White House offering to withdraw, warning the MFDP would otherwise be represented by 'commies' (Bill Kunstler and Arthur Kinoy)
claims.supporting_excerpt: What I found astounding was that he offered to withdraw in a letter to the White House. But he said, but I advise that I not withdraw because then it would be run by the commies. I mean Bill Kunstler and Arthur Kinoy would become counsel for the Freedom Democrats at the convention. (Segments 1072-1074)
claims.rationale: Direct statement of documentary finding from Todd's research.

claims.claim: LBJ's press conference timed against Hamer's testimony was pre-announced the day before with full camera coverage, not hastily called as Joseph L. Rauh Jr.'s account stated
claims.supporting_excerpt: The day before the Johnson told the press that he would be having a press conference in the middle of the afternoon and it would be full full press conference with camera. ... When he was watching the credentials committee testimony he was ready to cut it off. ... But everybody has written about it because of what Joseph L. Rauh Jr said. Joseph L. Rauh Jr told Anne Romaine that Johnson suddenly called the press conference. So everybody says hastily called hurriedly called. (Segments 1127-1133)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person research finding.

claims.claim: Representative Charles Diggs Jr. requested the list of credentials committee MFDP supporters from Bob Moses and Courtland Cox; the list was then used overnight to threaten members into changing votes
claims.supporting_excerpt: Courtland Cox has been very open about the requests that Rep. Charles Diggs Jr, who was a member of the Credentials Committee, prominent liberal Democratic Congressman from Michigan. ... Diggs requested the list of members of the Credentials Committee who supported the MFDP. And he made that request to Bob Moses in Courland Cox. ... And there were, I think they say, 15 people on the list. And that list was handed over. I'm not sure whether it was Sunday night or Monday night. But overnight calls were made to all the people on the list. And they were threatened with some loss of privilege or something. (Segments 1088-1094)
claims.rationale: Direct documentary-research statement; Todd cites Cox as the source.

claims.claim: Todd attended Stanford Law School after Freedom Summer 1964
claims.supporting_excerpt: And I said, I'll go to Stanford. (Segment 371) ... I drove across the country to Stanford to law school. (Segment 1165)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement.

claims.claim: Todd clerked for the National Lawyers Guild in Mississippi summer 1965, living in Tougaloo with Claudia Shropshire Morcom and helping coordinate volunteer lawyers evaluating Title III public-facility desegregation
claims.supporting_excerpt: So I was assigned to the National Lawyer's Guild and I lived in two glue with Claudia Shropshire Morcom who was there for one year. And we were she was the National Lawyers Guild was that summer of 1965 coordinating groups of volunteer lawyers who came to Mississippi to evaluate the need for litigation to open title three public facilities. (Segments 1184-1185)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement of assignment, housing partner, year, and substantive work.

claims.claim: Wiley A. Branton Sr. was executive director of the United Planning Organization in Washington during Todd's two-year tenure there
claims.supporting_excerpt: interesting also that I was at the United Planning Organization the exact two years that Wiley A. Branton Sr was there as the executive director (Segment 1203)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement.

claims.claim: Todd retired April 1, 2006 and produced a research paper called 'New Perceptions Atlantic City Revisited'
claims.supporting_excerpt: When I retired in April, April 1, 2006, seven years ago. (Segment 1016) ... I produced a research paper that with 250 pages called New Perceptions Atlantic City Revisited. (Segment 1036)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement of retirement date and paper title.

claims.claim: Todd argues there were only ~500 college-age volunteers in Freedom Summer, not 1,000 (with the additional ~500 being professionals dipping in for short periods)
claims.supporting_excerpt: By the way, there were only 500 of us. There were not 1,000 college-age volunteers. There were just a lot of professionals that dipped in for a couple of weeks. They make up the 1,000. (Segments 1270-1273)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person research finding.

claims.claim: Todd's closest brush with violence was a knife incident at a Greenville VFW dance
claims.supporting_excerpt: The closest thing I came to violence was being at a VFW dance at the beginning of the summer. ... The first guy comes out and pulls a knife. (Segments 1334, 1350)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person statement.

publication_decision.rationale: The main summary scores 88 accuracy and 89 quality -- both within 1-2 points of the 90/90 Smithsonian-grade threshold but neither meeting it -- and the citation audit shows 2 partially_supported claims (the Schwerner-Chaney-Goodman date and the Sharkey-as-third-county inference for Charles E. Cobb Jr.'s regional jurisdiction). Both partial-support items are honest interpretive judgments that the fail-closed gate is designed to surface for human review rather than auto-publish. The interview is high value and the summary is substantively faithful, but two specific points warrant a human's confirmation before publication: (a) confirming the canonical date for the SCG disappearance against the transcript's ambiguous Sunday-vs-Saturday wording, and (b) confirming whether the third county under Cobb's COFO regional responsibility was Sharkey (canonical) or another county that the speaker's mis-rendered audio obscured. Routing to the review queue is the correct outcome.
